content.text: Every AWS region has a short machine-readable code that follows a consistent pattern. These codes appear in API calls, CLI flags, ARNs, environment variables, CloudFormation parameters, and DNS endpoints. Understanding the naming scheme helps you read infrastructure code, catch misconfigurations, and navigate multi-region deployments confidently.

attrs.alt: AWS region code anatomy: geographic prefix, direction token, and ordinal number, with quick reference for common codes
attrs.title: AWS Region Code Structure

content.text: Every standard region code is composed of three parts joined by hyphens:

<geographic-prefix>-<direction-or-sublocation>-<ordinal>

Examples:
  us-east-1        → United States, East coast, first region
  eu-central-1     → Europe, Central, first region
  ap-southeast-2   → Asia Pacific, Southeast, second region
  us-gov-west-1    → GovCloud US, West, first region
  ap-east-1        → Asia Pacific, East (Hong Kong), first region
8 regions across Ireland, UK, Germany, France, Sweden, Italy, Spain, Zurich
Largest by region count; covers Singapore, Tokyo, Sydney, Seoul, Mumbai, Jakarta, and more

Region codes are embedded throughout every layer of AWS: API calls, CLI flags, resource ARNs, S3 bucket endpoints, SDK configuration, and IAM condition keys. Knowing the code for each region lets you read and write infrastructure code without consulting the console.
ARNs (Amazon Resource Names) embed the region code in their fourth segment:

arn:partition:service:region:account-id:resource

# Examples:
arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket                          # S3 is global — region is empty
arn:aws:ec2:us-east-1:123456789012:instance/i-1234
arn:aws:lambda:eu-west-1:123456789012:function:my-fn
arn:aws:dynamodb:ap-southeast-1:123456789012:table/users
arn:aws-cn:ec2:cn-north-1:123456789012:instance/i-1234  # China partition
arn:aws-us-gov:s3:::classified-bucket           # GovCloud partition
The partition prefix changes for special regions:
for GovCloud. IAM condition keys that restrict by region must use the correct partition's region codes.
Region Code in CLI and SDK

# CLI: explicit --region flag
aws ec2 describe-instances --region ap-northeast-1

# CLI: environment variable (takes precedence over config file)
export AWS_DEFAULT_REGION=eu-west-1
aws s3 ls  # uses eu-west-1

# CLI: profile-based region in ~/.aws/config
# [profile prod-eu]
# region = eu-west-1
aws s3 ls --profile prod-eu

# Boto3 (Python SDK): explicit region
import boto3
ec2 = boto3.client('ec2', region_name='us-west-2')

# Boto3: from environment variable AWS_DEFAULT_REGION
import os
os.environ['AWS_DEFAULT_REGION'] = 'us-west-2'
ec2 = boto3.client('ec2')  # picks up the env var
Most AWS service endpoints follow the pattern
. Knowing this pattern lets you construct endpoints programmatically and understand which region a connection targets:

# Standard endpoint pattern
https://<service>.<region>.amazonaws.com

# Examples:
https://ec2.us-east-1.amazonaws.com
https://s3.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com
https://dynamodb.ap-southeast-1.amazonaws.com
https://bedrock.us-east-1.amazonaws.com

# S3 path-style (legacy) vs virtual-hosted (current default)
https://s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/my-bucket/key      # path-style
https://my-bucket.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/key      # virtual-hosted

# China partition endpoints use .amazonaws.com.cn
https://ec2.cn-north-1.amazonaws.com.cn
as the prefix. These are isolated from the commercial
partition and accessible only to US persons with verified accounts:
FedRAMP High, DoD IL2-5, ITAR workloads
DR pair for GovCloud West; same compliance level
China regions use standard geographic codes but operate under the
partition, operated by licensed partners (Sinnet for cn-north-1, NWCD for cn-northwest-1). They are completely separate from global AWS accounts — you need a distinct China account to access them:

# China endpoints use .amazonaws.com.cn domain
aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --region cn-north-1 \
  --endpoint-url https://ec2.cn-north-1.amazonaws.com.cn

# ARNs in China partition
# arn:aws-cn:ec2:cn-north-1:123456789012:instance/i-1234

# Note: IAM ARNs for China accounts
# arn:aws-cn:iam::123456789012:user/alice
Using Region Codes in Infrastructure as Code

# CloudFormation pseudo-parameters for region-aware templates
Parameters:
  Environment:
    Type: String
    AllowedValues: [dev, prod]

Conditions:
  IsProduction: !Equals [!Ref Environment, prod]

Resources:
  MyBucket:
    Type: AWS::S3::Bucket
    Properties:
      # AWS::Region resolves to the region the stack is deployed into
      BucketName: !Sub "my-app-${AWS::Region}-${AWS::AccountId}"
      # Output: my-app-us-east-1-123456789012

  # Conditional resource: only in us-east-1
  PrimaryDatabase:
    Type: AWS::RDS::DBInstance
    Condition: IsProduction
    Properties:
      Tags:
        - Key: Region
          Value: !Ref AWS::Region

# List all enabled regions for your account
aws ec2 describe-regions \
  --query 'Regions[*].RegionName | sort(@)' \
  --output text

# List all regions including opt-in ones
aws ec2 describe-regions \
  --all-regions \
  --query 'sort_by(Regions, &RegionName)[*].{Region:RegionName,Status:OptInStatus}' \
  --output table

# Validate a region code before use in a script
REGION="eu-west-99"  # typo
if aws ec2 describe-regions --region-names "$REGION" --output text &>/dev/null; then
  echo "$REGION is valid"
else
  echo "$REGION is not a valid region" && exit 1
fi

# Get the display name for a region code
aws ssm get-parameter \
  --name "/aws/service/global-infrastructure/regions/${REGION}/longName" \
  --query 'Parameter.Value' \
  --output text \
  --region us-east-1 2>/dev/null || echo "Region not found"

The SSM Parameter Store at /aws/service/global-infrastructure/ is the canonical machine-readable registry for region codes, service availability, and display names. It's publicly readable from any account without authentication, making it ideal for CI/CD validation scripts.
Using a display name instead of the code
Using commercial endpoints for China regions

IAM policy condition keys use region codes to restrict what regions an action can target. This is how you enforce region-scoped permissions — allowing a role to deploy EC2 in eu-west-1 but nowhere else:
condition key matches the region code in the API request, not the region where the resource currently exists. Always pair it with
to exclude global services, or those services will be inadvertently denied.
Region Code in Environment Variables and Config Files

A well-structured project sets the region in one place and propagates it consistently through the stack. The following shows the full precedence chain the AWS SDK and CLI use when resolving the active region:
EC2 instance metadata (IMDSv2) — used automatically inside EC2, Lambda, ECS

# ~/.aws/config — named profiles with explicit region codes
[profile prod-us]
region = us-east-1
output = json

[profile prod-eu]
region = eu-west-1
output = json

[profile staging]
region = us-west-2
output = text

# Use a named profile
export AWS_PROFILE=prod-eu
aws ec2 describe-instances  # targets eu-west-1

# Or inline with --profile
aws s3 ls --profile prod-us

import os
import boto3
from botocore.config import Config

# 1. Read region from environment; fall back to a safe default
REGION = os.environ.get("AWS_DEFAULT_REGION", "us-east-1")

# 2. Apply retry and timeout configuration per region
# (Different regions may have different latency profiles)
config = Config(
    region_name=REGION,
    retries={"max_attempts": 3, "mode": "adaptive"},
    connect_timeout=5,
    read_timeout=60,
)

# 3. Create clients — all will target REGION
dynamodb = boto3.client("dynamodb", config=config)
lambda_client = boto3.client("lambda", config=config)

# 4. Verify the active region at runtime
session = boto3.session.Session()
print(f"Active region: {session.region_name}")
# Output: Active region: us-east-1
AZ Names: An Extension of the Naming Scheme
Availability Zone names extend the region code by appending a lowercase letter. An AZ named
is the first AZ in the us-east-1 region. However, the letter suffix is not standardized across accounts —
in your account may map to a different physical data center than

# AZ name vs AZ ID — names are account-scoped, IDs are universal
aws ec2 describe-availability-zones \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'AvailabilityZones[*].{Name:ZoneName,ID:ZoneId,State:State}' \
  --output table

# Output:
# -----------------------------------------------
# |     DescribeAvailabilityZones               |
# +------+-------------+-----------+            |
# | ID   | Name        | State     |            |
# +------+-------------+-----------+            |
# |use1-az1| us-east-1a| available |            |
# |use1-az2| us-east-1b| available |            |
# |use1-az4| us-east-1c| available |            |
# +------+-------------+-----------+            |

# AZ IDs (use1-az1) are consistent across accounts
# Use AZ IDs (not names) when sharing resources across accounts via RAM
Yes — same physical region for all accounts
No — letter suffix is randomized per account to spread load
Yes — stable physical identifier; use for cross-account sharing via RAM
Applications running inside AWS (on EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS) can discover their current region from the instance metadata service without any hardcoded configuration:

import urllib.request
import boto3

# Method 1: IMDSv2 (secure, preferred inside EC2)
def get_region_from_imds() -> str:
    # Step 1: Get IMDSv2 token
    token_req = urllib.request.Request(
        "http://169.254.169.254/latest/api/token",
        method="PUT",
        headers={"X-aws-ec2-metadata-token-ttl-seconds": "21600"}
    )
    with urllib.request.urlopen(token_req, timeout=2) as r:
        token = r.read().decode()

    # Step 2: Use token to read region
    region_req = urllib.request.Request(
        "http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/placement/region",
        headers={"X-aws-ec2-metadata-token": token}
    )
    with urllib.request.urlopen(region_req, timeout=2) as r:
        return r.read().decode()

# Method 2: Boto3 session (works everywhere — reads env var or config)
def get_region_from_boto3() -> str:
    session = boto3.session.Session()
    return session.region_name

# Method 3: Lambda — region is in the context object
# def handler(event, context):
#     region = context.invoked_function_arn.split(':')[3]
#     return region
— learn the geographic prefixes and you can decode any code at a glance.
Region codes appear in ARNs, CLI flags, SDK configuration, service endpoints, and IaC templates — consistency matters across all of them.
(Terraform) instead of hardcoding region codes in templates.
Validate region codes in CI/CD with
or the SSM global-infrastructure parameter store before deploying.
